title: A peaceful game of thrones
html_body:
Today in Science and science: If you give a monkey VR, a quantum leap in genomic computing, and more
Brought to you by Danaher Corporation
View this email in your browser
16 April 2026
Today’s Protostar is Arri Coomarasamy, finalist for this year’s BioInnovation Institute & Science Translational Medicine Prize for Innovations in Women’s Health. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including what happens when you put monkeys in a virtual reality world and lessons in peaceful transitions of power from mole rats.
Computer Science  |  News from Science
Viral genome loaded into quantum computer
A surge in DNA sequencing has left researchers awash in data, straining even the most powerful classical computers and prompting a search for new ways to analyze genomes. Now, scientists report an early step toward one possibility: quantum computing.

In work announced last week, a team encoded the complete genome of the hepatitis D virus—the smallest known human virus—into a quantum computer. The result does not yet demonstrate any computational advantage, but it tackles a fundamental hurdle: getting biological data into qubits in the first place. “That’s an essential step,” explained quantum algorithms researcher Guglielmo Mazzola. “If you want to do genomic processing, you need to first put the data in.”

Still, researchers caution that practical applications remain distant. Today’s quantum machines are too small and error-prone to handle larger genomes or perform meaningful analyses. And it remains unclear whether quantum approaches will ultimately outperform highly optimized classical methods. For now, the work highlights growing interest in quantum genomics. “We’re at the start of the journey,” said James McCafferty, Wellcome Sanger’s chief information officer.
Read the full story
Neuroscience  |  Science Advances
If you give a monkey VR
Monkeys navigated this VR world by thought alone.  Saussus et al./Science Advances (2026)
Upon reading Roald Dahl’s Matilda, countless children have stared intently at water glasses, hoping their thoughts could tip them over. Researchers have been working on a similar concept: brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) that translate neural activity into real-world commands.

BCIs are especially promising for people with brain damage; researchers have already had success in experiments to control computer cursors, prosthetic arms, and wheelchairs. So, to test the technology in more realistic environments, they turned to a 3D virtual reality (VR) system.

The team implanted three rhesus monkeys with electrodes in three motor regions of the brain. In a VR space, their tasks included moving a spherical object and avoiding obstacles on a path. The researchers found that the monkeys got better at the tasks over time, and were able to demonstrate precise control over objects and continuous navigation. Such qualities would be essential for eventual human use in real-world environments like a person’s cluttered house.

Future VR-trained BCIs “could substantially improve the quality of life for paralyzed patients by enabling them to perform daily tasks with greater independence,” wrote the authors.
Read the paper
Animal Behavior  |  Science Advances
A peaceful game of thrones
Naked mole rat colonies run a tight ship. They are eusocial mammals, organized around a single breeding queen like bees or ants, while the rest of the colony takes on nonreproductive roles. If that queen dies, her daughters typically fight, often violently, to take her place. But a new study published in Science Advances shows that succession doesn’t always have to be so ruthless.

Researchers followed a single captive colony, known as the “Amigos,” which was led by their queen Teré for more than 6 years. The team first increased colony density, a stressor known to affect reproduction in rodents. As the colony grew crowded, Teré continued to get pregnant and give birth, but none of her pups survived. Despite the reproductive failure, no aggression broke out and no subordinate females stepped in.

To further test how different stressors affect reproduction, the team relocated the colony to a new facility. Even though other environmental conditions were kept consistent, the move triggered a months-long pause in Teré’s reproduction. During that lull, one of her daughters, Alexandria, began to breed, with pregnancies overlapping between mother and daughter, notably without any fighting.

After Alexandria died from a pregnancy complication, another daughter, Arwen, took her place. Over time, Arwen became the colony’s primary breeder, while Teré stopped reproducing altogether. Thus, the colony moved from a single queen to a brief period of shared rule (known as plural breeding), then back to a single monarch—all without the usual “wars of succession” observed when a queen is removed.

The findings suggest that naked mole rat societies are more flexible than previously thought and can separate reproductive failure from social instability. “Our study reveals a ‘hidden’ side of reproductive organization in naked mole rat colonies,” said co-first author Alexandria Schraibman in a statement, opening what she describes as “an entirely new line of inquiry.”
Read the paper
SPONSORED
Danaher combines AI-driven discovery with a proven strategic framework to expand what’s possible for the future of healthcare.
Read Now
Protostar
Courtesy of Arri Coomarasamy
Arri Coomarasamy
Professor of Gynaecology and Reproductive Medicine, University of Oxford

Coomarasamy, A. E-MOTIVE: Helping to combat severe maternal bleeding during childbirth. Science Translational Medicine 18 (2026). 10.1126/scitranslmed.aeg8044
In 30 years of practicing obstetrics in the United Kingdom, Arri Coomarasamy has never lost a patient to postpartum hemorrhage. In low-income countries, most obstetricians can’t say the same. As a professor of gynecology and reproductive medicine at the University of Oxford and founding director of the WHO Collaborating Centre for Global Women’s Health, Coomarasamy has spent nearly two decades working on postpartum hemorrhage, the excessive bleeding after childbirth that is the leading cause of maternal death worldwide. Every 2 minutes, somewhere in the world, a woman dies giving birth and Coomarasamy believes most of those deaths are preventable.

His landmark E-MOTIVE trial was conducted across 80 hospitals in Kenya, Nigeria, South Africa, and Tanzania and involved more than 200,000 women. The results, published in the New England Journal of Medicine in 2023, showed that early, accurate diagnosis combined with immediate bundle treatment reduced severe hemorrhage by 60% . The World Health Organization has since adopted the approach as a global recommendation, leading to a rapid and dramatic reduction in childbirth deaths in some areas since implementing the intervention.

This spring, Coomarasamy was named a finalist for the 2026 BioInnovation Institute & Science Translational Medicine Prize for Innovations in Women’s Health, recognizing his work on E-MOTIVE; the honor includes his essay on the intervention being published in Science Translational Medicine alongside a cash prize of $10,000.

ScienceAdviser spoke with Coomarasamy about his work. Below is that conversation, edited for brevity.

How did you end up working on postpartum hemorrhage?
I was in Malawi about two decades ago. While I was there, I visited a delivery suite. A typical delivery suite has all the rooms labeled, but one locked room had no label on it. The staff were reluctant to show me inside that room, but I persuaded them. To my shock, it turned out to be a mortuary on the delivery suite. There were three women in that room, two of them had died of postpartum hemorrhage. I walked out that room a different person. At the time I was actually training to become a specialist in IVF, in reproductive medicine, but that experience had a massive impact on the way I pursued my professional life, and the focus of my work.

What was the key insight behind E-MOTIVE?
The really profound insight we had was that there was a massive diagnostic problem. As many as 50% of women who were having a postpartum hemorrhage weren’t being diagnosed. Women were in the delivery suite, they’d given birth, and they were bleeding excessively, and we didn’t realize it! We had been relying on subjective visual assessment to estimate the amount of blood loss. A woman’s blood loss could have been between 200 milliliters and 1000 milliliters; we just didn’t have a good way of knowing.

So we said: Let’s stop estimating; let’s measure the blood loss. That’s where the idea of using a calibrated blood collection drape came in, a plastic bag placed under the woman after childbirth, with a funnel shape at the bottom and calibration markings, so you can say at any given point how much she’s lost and when we need to do something. That massively improved the diagnosis, and once that was sorted out, we worked on giving all the effective treatments in our armory very quickly. Previously, treatment was mostly sequential: give one treatment, wait and see if the patient stopped bleeding, then give the second, then the third. The trouble is that time was being lost that way. So, we instituted a treatment bundle—uterine massage, oxytocin drugs, tranexamic acid, intravenous fluids, and examining to find the cause of bleeding—all at once. We were looking for a relative improvement of 25%, but we found a 60% reduction. This shows you that the consequences of postpartum hemorrhage can be avoided.

How did E-MOTIVE then get distributed in hospitals around the world?
As researchers, we publish in the New England Journal of Medicine and then we think, okay, well, job done! But it’s actually only the beginning of helping the evidence reach people. We were really mindful of not letting there be a gap between discovery and delivery, so we worked with WHO, with the International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics (FIGO) and the International Confederation of Midwives (ICM), and they jointly issued a recommendation saying early diagnosis with a calibrated drape and bundle treatment is the way to move forward. That meant we were able to work with funders, health ministries, other policymakers to start implementing this in their own countries. We wanted to make sure the calibrated drape was available everywhere, so funders like the Gates Foundation worked with local manufacturers on that. There are now provinces in Kenya where they haven’t reported a single death over a year since they implemented the E-MOTIVE intervention.

What’s next for your research?
E-MOTIVE addressed postpartum hemorrhage at vaginal birth, but it doesn’t tackle the problem at cesarean section, where the risk of hemorrhage is more than double, and the risk of dying from it is also higher. So, we are pursuing a program called E-MASTER, modeled on E-MOTIVE but for cesarean section. We’re using a technology called Triton, for colorimetric assessment of blood loss, which allows you to hold a blood-soaked swab up to a camera, and an AI algorithm works out how much blood is on it from the area of redness and color density. It tallies up and gives the surgeon an ongoing blood loss volume throughout the procedure. Right now, we’re validating the technology, and once that’s completed we would move on to a big trial, hopefully just like the E-MOTIVE trial.

How do you hold the emotional weight of this work
When you look at trial data, you get statistics. Mortality numbers will come through into my in-tray, and by using words like “mortality” we kind of sanitize it—it’s just a number on an Excel sheet. And then you sit and think: These numbers are women whose names I don’t know. They probably have young children. They probably went into the hospital with hope, to bring back home a newborn. And they won’t be doing that. I’ve got, by the grace of God, three beautiful daughters, and I hope they will one day have children, and the births will be safe. But I know this is not the case for many, many women out there. I suppose the way that I deal with it is by saying: My team are part of the solution. And I just try to take comfort from that.

What do you do when you’re not in the clinic or the lab?
About a year ago I had a heart attack, completely unexpectedly, while at the WHO headquarters in Geneva. There I was, at a meeting about the importance of early diagnosis of postpartum hemorrhage, but needing medical care myself. I had an early diagnosis, got rushed to the hospital and had the stents put in, in record time. Now I try to have a healthy living regimen. These days I walk for an hour or two every day, spend lots of time with my kids, and listen to audiobooks. I’m currently listening to Four Thousand Weeks , which is about the finitude of life. You would think it’s a depressing book, but it’s not. (Laughs.) It’s about knowing your time is limited and making the most of it. Like a holiday; if you know you have two weeks somewhere wonderful, you just approach it differently.
Read the Prize Essay
Et Cetera
Old school immunity
Countless millennia ago, bacteria evolved sophisticated systems to thwart their viral enemies. Some of these can be found in our own immune system today. “Seeing that the rules of host-virus interactions are unchanged over billions of years is a really hard thing to digest,” said one microbiologist.
Read more at Quanta Magazine
An eggciting find
Back in the Permian period, synapsids ruled the land. Most perished about 250 million years ago in The Great Dying; the few that survived would give rise to mammals. Now, the first non-mammalian fossilized synapsid egg is giving paleontologists clues as to how these ancient kin of ours reproduced.
PLOS ONE Paper  |  Read more at Scientific American
Bay of the dead
A 7-year study found one in five gray whales that enter San Fransisco Bay die there—and that’s likely an underestimate, researchers said. The animals seem to be stopping in dangerous waters because they’re getting hungry during their 16,000-kilometer migration from the Arctic to warmer breeding grounds and back. “If you’re desperate, and you go into San Francisco Bay, it looks like you’ve got a really, really high chance of not making it back out,” said one expert, “and it’s really only necessary because they’re not getting what they need in the Arctic.”
Frontiers in Marine Science Paper  |  Read more at Science News
47 million
The number of galaxies now mapped by the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument
It took the DESI team 5 years to compile the map, which ended up with 13 million more galaxies than expected.
News from Science
Last but not least
My pup Arya may not know hundreds of words. But she’s smart in the ways that count. As New York Times reporter Emily Anthes wrote about her dog Watson, she’s got loads of skills suited to her highly specialized ecological niche—and is, objectively, a very, very good girl.
Christie Wilcox, Editor, ScienceAdviser

With contributions from Eric Hand, Hannah Richter, and Ana Georgescu

items.title: the complete genome of the hepatitis D virus—the smallest known human virus—into a quantum computer
the monkeys got better at the tasks over time
succession doesn’t always have to be so ruthless
early, accurate diagnosis combined with immediate bundle treatment reduced severe hemorrhage by 60%
